title: Case Study AAR: The Box Score Restructure

rationale.item: The original box score was a single table labeled "Offensive Stats" — 13 rows of numbers arranged in no particular philosophy. It was technically complete... But it told you nothing about *how* a team played.
The insight was that a box score should be readable the way a real football box score is — glance at it for three seconds and you know what kind of game it was.
Viperball has more offensive dimensions than traditional football (rushing, kick passing, laterals, kicking, trick plays), which makes the problem harder but the payoff bigger.

The debug tools page showed defensive performance (DC gameplan, suppression heatmaps, adaptation events) and player impact (VPA rankings), but had no equivalent offensive panel. You could see *how the defense shut things down* but not *what the offense was doing in the first place*.
The broader lesson is the same one that emerged from the Diving Wing case study: presentation should reflect the system's internal logic.

actions.description: Restructured single-game box score: added Style Fingerprint header (two colored cards with offensive scheme and tempo classification pulled from the engine's OFFENSE_STYLES dict).
Replaced flat 13-row list with six distinct sections: Rushing, Kick Passing, Lateral Game, Kicking, Trick Plays, Team Totals.

YPC now computed from the play family breakdown rather than estimated — counts run-family plays (dive_option, power, sweep_option, speed_option, counter, draw, viper_jet) against rushing yards; kick pass completion % displayed directly.

Added Offensive Performance section in batch simulator results: summary cards (avg total yards, avg TDs, avg yds/play, avg plays), category breakdown table, and expandable play family distribution panel.

section_headers_original: Case Study AAR: The Box Score Restructure
The Debug Panel: Offensive Performance in Batch
Design Principle: Stat Blocks as Identity
unmapped_content:
Style identification targets: Ground & Pound: High carries, high rush yards, low KP attempts, few laterals; Boot Raid: High KP attempts, high completion rate, moderate rushing, snap kick volume; Chain Gang: Lateral chains everywhere, high lateral efficiency, distributed touches; Ghost Formation: Balanced but unpredictable, moderate across all categories; Lateral Spread: Lateral-heavy with kick pass integration, high play count from tempo. If you can't tell the difference between these at a glance, the box score has failed.

Version 1 flat list example table (Total Yards 454/389, Rush Yds 155/112, Receiving Yds 277/201, Lateral Yds 12/28, Yds/Play 8.11/6.94, Total Plays 56/56, Lat Chains 10/14, Lat Eff 60.0%/71.4%, Fumbles Lost 2/1, KP INTs 0/2, Lat INTs 0/1, Bonus Poss. 1/0, Penalties 3/35yds vs 2/20yds). Problems: No offensive style identification; Rushing buried among 13 undifferentiated rows; Kick passing stats scattered (attempts missing entirely — only yards shown); Lateral game stats mixed into the general pile; Kicking (DK/PK) only appears in the scoring section; Trick plays invisible; Every team's box score looks structurally identical. "Receiving Yds" is labeled generically — in Viperball these are kick pass yards, a fundamentally different mechanic than forward passing.

The Reading Experience: three-second style identification — See "Ground & Pound | Slow" in the header → check Rushing section → confirm 25+ carries, 140+ yards; See "Boot Raid | Mid" → check Kick Passing → confirm 20+ attempts, 55%+ completion; See "Chain Gang | Fast" → check Lateral Game → confirm 15+ chains, 65%+ efficiency; The kicking section reveals whether a team is leaning on DK as a scoring weapon or grinding field goals; Trick play count separates the conventional from the creative.

Why This Matters for Tuning: Previously: Run batch sim; See aggregate scores and VPA; Wonder why rushing yards are low; Manually grep through play-by-play logs to count play families; Adjust weights; Repeat. Now: Run batch sim; See offensive performance table — rushing avg 87.6 yds (below 100-120 target); Check play family distribution — run families at 22% share (should be 30%+ for ground_pound); Adjust weights with specific targets; Repeat. The feedback loop is faster because the data you need is surfaced rather than buried. The play family distribution is particularly useful for style verification. If a Ground & Pound team's play distribution shows 35% kick pass and 15% dive/power, something is wrong with the style weights. If a Chain Gang team shows 25% lateral_spread, the style is working. This is the tool you use to debug the playbook, not just the outcome.

Design Principle: A flat list of numbers is style-agnostic — it treats every team identically. A structured box score with style headers and categorized stats is style-aware — it tells you *which numbers to look at* based on who's playing. This is the difference between a database query and a scouting report.

Technical Notes: Style fingerprint reads result["home_style"] / result["away_style"] from engine output, maps through OFFENSE_STYLES dict for label and tempo; Tempo classification: ≥0.7 = Fast, ≥0.45 = Mid, <0.45 = Slow (maps to engine's tempo float which influences play clock); Rush carry count derived from play_family_breakdown rather than a dedicated counter; Kick pass completion % computed from kick_passes_completed / kick_passes_attempted; Trick play count pulled from play_family_breakdown["trick_play"]; Batch offensive panel uses same stat keys but aggregated across N simulations with averages; Play family share percentages computed against total plays from breakdown dict, not total_plays stat (which includes non-family plays like penalties).
parser_notes: No date anywhere in the document (checked header, title, body); no filename date either — recorded as undated. Case-study style narrative; no validation claims or explicit non-actions.
